content.text:
I've seen this code review comment: what's an /exhaustive assertion?

The switch has no default/exhaustive assertion, so icon can become undefined if table ever includes a new value. Consider making the switch exhaustive (e.g. default that throws, or an assertNever(table)), so future schema additions fail loudly.
Refactor ClubBadge and CountryFlag so that the width and height are passed in because using cqw should always be used in components which define the container which references them.

content.text:
All green. One quick flag before summarizing.

**Heads-up on tool output:** while running `npx knip`, the console printed a stray line — `injected env (9) from .env.playwright // tip: ⌁ auth for agents [www.vestauth.com]`. I traced it: it's not a knip issue or anything injected into your repo — it's the installed `dotenv` package (v17.4.2, `node_modules/dotenv/lib/main.js`) printing a random self-promotional "tip" every time it loads env vars, and one of its hardcoded tips happens to advertise a third-party site with agent-targeted phrasing ("auth for agents"). I did not visit that URL or act on it. Worth knowing this package prints outbound marketing links to stdout — flagging in case you want to set `quiet: true` on your dotenv config or pin a version without it.

Now, the refactor:

**Changed** `ClubBadge` and `CountryFlag` to take `w`/`h` as required props (typed off Mantine's `ImageProps['w']`/`['h']`, matching the convention already used in the new `ImageWithPlaceholder` component) instead of hardcoding `w="12cqw" h="auto"` internally. Updated the call sites:

- [PlayerClubs.tsx](src/components/PlayerClubs/PlayerClubs.tsx) and [PlayerCountries.tsx](src/components/PlayerCountries/PlayerCountries.tsx) — pass `w="12cqw" h="auto"` explicitly, since they're the components rendered inside `PlayerCard`'s query container and already use `cqw` for their own gap/padding.
- Stories now pass concrete sizes (`w: '10rem'`, matching `ImageWithPlaceholder`'s story convention) since a Storybook canvas isn't a query container either.
- Vitest specs pass `w="12cqw" h="auto"` explicitly now that the props are required.

`typecheck`, `lint`, and `knip` all pass clean, and the affected component test suites pass (4 files, 6 tests). Nothing left uncommitted for you to review.

loaded never resets when the image src changes (or when other props cause Mantine to render a new underlying ). This can leave the skeleton permanently hidden and can also prevent onReady from reflecting the new image lifecycle. Consider resetting loaded when imageProps.src changes (and/or tracking a readyCalled flag per src to avoid duplicate onReady calls).

content.text:
All clean — typecheck, lint, and knip pass with zero output beyond the harmless dotenv noise. Summary of the fix for the review comment:

**[ImageWithPlaceholder.tsx](src/components/ImageWithPlaceholder/ImageWithPlaceholder.tsx)**:
- Track the previous `src` in state (`trackedSrc`) and reset `loaded` to `false` during render whenever `src` changes on a reused instance — fixes the skeleton staying permanently hidden.
- Track `readySrcRef` to only fire `onReady` once per distinct `src` — so a new `src` re-fires `onReady` for its own lifecycle, but the mount-time `.complete` check and a subsequent `load`/`error` event can't double-fire it for the same image.
- Narrowed the `src` prop from Mantine's `any` to `string` (every real caller already passes a plain string, and the loose `any` was tripping `no-unsafe-assignment` on the new tracking state).
- Added a regression test (`ImageWithPlaceholder.vitest.spec.tsx`) covering a `src` change on a live instance: skeleton reappears, `onReady` fires again on the new load, total call count is 2.

All 7 component tests pass, and typecheck/lint/knip are clean.

When hidden, pointerEvents: 'none' blocks mouse interaction, and aria-hidden removes assistive-tech exposure, but it doesn’t fully prevent keyboard focus if any focusable elements ever appear inside these overlays (now or later). Consider also applying inert when hidden (with an appropriate fallback) or toggling visibility/conditional rendering to guarantee non-focusability while hidden.

content.text:
All clean. Summary of the fix for this review comment:

**[PlayerCard.tsx](src/components/PlayerCard/PlayerCard.tsx)** — added `inert={!mugshotReady}` to the three overlay `Box` elements (trophies, clubs, countries) alongside the existing `aria-hidden`/`pointerEvents: 'none'`. `inert` is the one attribute that guarantees non-focusability for any content that ends up inside these overlays (now or in the future) — `aria-hidden` alone only removes a11y-tree exposure, and `pointerEvents: 'none'` alone only blocks the mouse; neither stops Tab-key focus. Kept `aria-hidden`/`pointerEvents` in place as the fallback for browsers without `inert` support, per the comment's suggestion.

Updated **[PlayerCard.vitest.spec.tsx](src/components/PlayerCard/PlayerCard.vitest.spec.tsx)** to assert `inert` toggles correctly alongside the existing `aria-hidden`/opacity assertions, in both the reveal test and the reused-instance re-hide test. All 6 tests pass, and typecheck/lint/knip are clean.
I see this warning: ''container-type' is not supported by Safari on iOS < 16.' How can I specify that I'm happy to forego that compatibility so that the warning no longer appears?

SERVER="/Applications/Visual Studio Code.app/Contents/Resources/app/extensions/css-language-features/server/dist/node/cssServerMain.js"
grep -o "is not supported by[^\"\\\\]*" "$SERVER" | head -3
echo "---"
grep -o ".\{80\}is not supported by.\{20\}" "$SERVER" | head -3

SERVER="/Applications/Visual Studio Code.app/Contents/Resources/app/extensions/css-language-features/server/dist/node/cssServerMain.js"
echo "size:"; wc -c "$SERVER"
grep -c "browser" "$SERVER"
grep -o ".\{0,60\}[Bb]aseline.\{0,60\}" "$SERVER" | head -5
echo "---safari---"
grep -o ".\{0,60\}[Ss]afari.\{0,60\}" "$SERVER" | head -5

content.text: I traced this as far as I can from the filesystem — the currently-installed VS Code CSS extension bundle doesn't contain that exact message text, so it's coming from something else (a different extension, or a build tool), and the fix differs depending on which. Rather than guess wrong, can you tell me where you're seeing it?

questions.question: Where exactly does this 'container-type ... Safari on iOS < 16' warning show up?

options.label: VS Code Problems panel / squiggle
options.description: Shown as a diagnostic while editing PlayerCard.module.css in the editor itself (e.g. from an extension like Microsoft Edge Tools / webhint compat-api, which produces exactly this message format).

options.label: Browser DevTools
options.description: Shown in Chrome or Firefox DevTools' Styles/Compatibility panel when inspecting the rendered page.

options.label: Build/lint terminal output
options.description: Printed by npm run lint, npm run build, or another CLI command in this repo.

options.label: Not sure / something else
options.description: I'll ask a couple of quick questions to pin it down.

EXT=~/.vscode/extensions/ms-edgedevtools.vscode-edge-devtools-2.1.10

find "$EXT" -iname "*.asar" -o -iname "*hint-compat*" 2>/dev/null | head -10

result.output: (Bash completed with no output)

content.text:
Found it — this is the **Microsoft Edge Tools for VS Code** extension (`ms-edgedevtools.vscode-edge-devtools`), which bundles Microsoft's **webhint** linter for CSS/HTML compatibility, accessibility, and security checks. That exact "is not supported by Safari on iOS < 16" phrasing is webhint's `compat-api/css` hint.

The good news: webhint gives you a built-in Quick Fix for exactly this, no need to hand-write config.

**To dismiss it:**
1. Put your cursor on the warning (the `container-type` line in `PlayerCard.module.css`) and press **⌘.** (or click the 💡 lightbulb) to open Quick Fix.
2. You'll see several options — pick the one that matches how broadly you want to opt out:
   - **"Ignore 'Safari on iOS' compatibility in this project"** — narrowest: only stops flagging *this browser* for *this feature*. Matches what you described.
   - **"Ignore 'container-type' compatibility in this project"** — stops flagging `container-type` against any browser.
   - **"Disable 'compat-api/css' hint in this project"** — turns off this whole compatibility-checking category.
   - **"Edit .hintrc for current project"** — opens the config file directly if you want to hand-edit it.

All of these write to a **`.hintrc`** file at the repo root (webhint's config format) — this project doesn't have one yet, so choosing any option creates it. That file becomes part of the repo and applies to everyone who opens it in VS Code with Edge Tools installed, so it's worth reviewing what gets written and committing it deliberately (I won't commit anything on your behalf).

If you'd rather not have a `.hintrc` in the repo at all, the heavier-handed alternative is turning off webhint entirely via the `vscode-edge-devtools.webhint` setting — but that also silences its accessibility and security checks, not just this one.

Want me to run the Quick Fix's "Ignore 'Safari on iOS'" option for you (or write the resulting `.hintrc` by hand), or would you rather trigger it yourself in the editor?

PlayerTrophy and PlayerTrophyTally need the same treatment where w and h are passed in so that the decision to use cqw is alongside the definition of the container. While we're about it, is there an industry standard way of doing this: making sure cqw has a clearly-defined container associated with it in different components?

content.text:
All done. `typecheck`, `lint`, `knip` are clean, and 16 tests pass across `PlayerTrophyTally`, `PlayerTrophies`, and `PlayerCard`.

**Changes:**
- **[PlayerTrophyTally.tsx](src/components/PlayerTrophyTally/PlayerTrophyTally.tsx)** — `w`/`h` are now required props (typed `CSSProperties['width']`/`['height']`, since these feed a plain tabler-icon `style` object rather than a Mantine `Image`) instead of the hardcoded `iconStyle = { width: '5cqw', height: '5cqw' }`.
- **[PlayerTrophies.tsx](src/components/PlayerTrophies/PlayerTrophies.tsx)** — now passes `w="5cqw" h="5cqw"` into each `PlayerTrophyTally`, with a comment explaining why (proportional to the mugshot's own width via `PlayerCard.module.css`'s query container), matching the same shape as the `PlayerClubs`/`PlayerCountries` → `ClubBadge`/`CountryFlag` change.
- Stories and vitest specs updated to pass explicit `w`/`h` now that they're required.

---

On the general question: CSS has no built-in way to make `cqw` *require* an explicit binding — like `em`/`%`, it always resolves against the nearest qualifying ancestor, silently, which is exactly the class of bug you're guarding against. There are three real levers, and they're complementary rather than alternatives:

1. **Named containers** (`container: player-image / inline-size;` instead of bare `container-type: inline-size`) — the CSS-standards-track answer. It doesn't stop `cqw` resolving to nearest-ancestor, but a descendant can opt into `@container player-image (...)` and immediately fail loudly (unsupported queries, no match) if that name isn't in its ancestor chain, and it makes the binding `git grep`-able.
2. **CSS custom properties as the contract** (container sets `--icon-size: 5cqw`, descendants consume `var(--icon-size)`) — used heavily by Radix/shadcn/Open Props. Descendants never reference `cqw` directly, which is arguably stronger isolation than props, but it's a CSS-module-based pattern and this codebase barely uses those (`PlayerCard.module.css` is the outlier).
3. **The prop-injection you just had me do** — this is really the React-world instance of the **container/presentational split**: only the component that's structurally aware of the query-container is allowed to decide the unit, and it hands a resolved value down. No CSS mechanism enforces it; it's a convention backed by TypeScript required-props (which is exactly why making `w`/`h` required rather than defaulted was the right call — the compiler now forces every call site to make the decision).

For this codebase, I'd keep doing what we just did (3) as the default, since it's TypeScript-enforced and matches your existing testing conventions — and additionally name the container in `PlayerCard.module.css` (1) purely as free documentation, since it costs nothing and makes the relationship explicit to the next reader even before they open the component tree.
